flashcards.q: Define the magnetic effect of electric current.
flashcards.a: The phenomenon where an electric current flowing through a conductor produces a magnetic field around it.

flashcards.q: Who discovered that electric current produces a magnetic field?
flashcards.a: Hans Christian Oersted, a Danish scientist, in 1820.

flashcards.q: What is an electromagnet?
flashcards.a: A current-carrying coil that behaves as a magnet.

flashcards.q: How can we detect the presence of a magnetic field?
flashcards.a: Using a magnetic compass—the compass needle deflects when placed near a magnetic field.

flashcards.q: What happens to an electromagnet when the electric current is stopped?
flashcards.a: The electromagnet loses its magnetic properties immediately—the magnetic field disappears.

flashcards.q: Does an electromagnet have poles like a bar magnet?
flashcards.a: Yes, an electromagnet has two poles: North and South, similar to a bar magnet.

flashcards.q: What happens when you reverse the direction of electric current in an electromagnet?
flashcards.a: The polarity of the electromagnet reverses—the North pole becomes South and the South pole becomes North.

flashcards.q: How does inserting an iron core into a coil affect its magnetic strength?
flashcards.a: Inserting an iron core significantly increases the strength of the electromagnet because iron amplifies the magnetic field.

flashcards.q: How does increasing the number of turns in a coil affect electromagnet strength?
flashcards.a: More turns produce a stronger electromagnet because the magnetic fields from each turn add together.

flashcards.q: How does increasing the electric current affect the strength of an electromagnet?
flashcards.a: Greater current produces a stronger magnetic field, making the electromagnet stronger.

flashcards.q: What are lifting electromagnets used for?
flashcards.a: Lifting and moving heavy metal objects in factories and scrap yards; controlled by switching current ON and OFF.

flashcards.q: Define the heating effect of electric current.
flashcards.a: The generation of heat in a conductor when electric current flows through it due to resistance.

flashcards.q: Why does a conductor become hot when electric current flows through it?
flashcards.a: The conductor offers resistance to current flow; this resistance converts electrical energy into heat energy.

flashcards.q: Why is nichrome wire preferred in heating appliances over copper wire?
flashcards.a: Nichrome wire has higher resistance than copper, so it generates more heat for the same current.

flashcards.q: What are heating elements in electric appliances?
flashcards.a: Rods or coils of high-resistance wire (usually nichrome) that generate heat when electric current flows through them.

flashcards.q: Name three household appliances that work on the heating effect of electric current.
flashcards.a: Electric heater, electric kettle, electric iron (also acceptable: hair dryer, immersion rod, electric stove, toaster).

flashcards.q: What factors determine the amount of heat generated in a wire?
flashcards.a: Material of the wire, thickness, length, magnitude of electric current, and duration of current flow.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between heat, current, resistance, and time?
flashcards.a: Heat = I² × R × t (Heat is proportional to the square of current, resistance, and time).

flashcards.q: True or False: A thinner wire generates more heat than a thicker wire of the same material when carrying the same current.
flashcards.a: True, because thinner wires have higher resistance.

flashcards.q: Why must electrical circuits have appropriate wires and safety devices?
flashcards.a: To prevent overheating, which can melt plastic components, damage appliances, or cause fires.

flashcards.q: Define a Voltaic cell.
flashcards.a: An early electric cell consisting of two different metal electrodes and an electrolyte that generates electricity through chemical reactions.

flashcards.q: What are the electrodes in a Voltaic cell?
flashcards.a: Two metal plates made of different materials (e.g., copper and iron, or zinc and copper) that participate in chemical reactions.

flashcards.q: What is an electrolyte in a cell?
flashcards.a: A liquid (weak acid or salt solution) or paste that enables chemical reactions between the electrodes to produce electricity.

flashcards.q: Who invented the Voltaic cell?
flashcards.a: Alessandro Volta, based on observations by Luigi Galvani in the late 1700s.

flashcards.q: How does a Voltaic cell produce electric current?
flashcards.a: Chemical reactions between the metal electrodes and electrolyte cause electrons to accumulate at one terminal, creating an electrical potential difference.

flashcards.q: What is a dry cell?
flashcards.a: A practical electric cell with a paste-like electrolyte (instead of liquid) inside a zinc container with a central carbon rod.

flashcards.q: What are the terminals of a dry cell?
flashcards.a: Negative terminal: zinc container; Positive terminal: carbon rod covered with a metal cap.

flashcards.q: Can a dry cell be recharged?
flashcards.a: No, a dry cell is single-use; once the chemicals are consumed, it becomes 'dead' and must be discarded.

flashcards.q: What is a rechargeable battery?
flashcards.a: A battery that can be charged and discharged multiple times before eventually wearing out.

flashcards.q: Name the most common type of modern rechargeable battery.
flashcards.a: Lithium-ion (Li-ion) battery.

flashcards.q: What is the main advantage of rechargeable batteries over dry cells?
flashcards.a: They can be used multiple times by recharging, reducing waste and saving money over time.

flashcards.q: True or False: Rechargeable batteries last forever and never need replacement.
flashcards.a: False, after repeated charging cycles, they eventually wear out and lose capacity.

flashcards.q: Why must used batteries be recycled in e-waste facilities?
flashcards.a: They contain hazardous materials like acids and toxic metals (lead, cadmium, lithium) that harm the environment if thrown in regular garbage.

flashcards.q: What hazardous materials are found in batteries?
flashcards.a: Acids, lead, cadmium, nickel, lithium, and other toxic metals.

flashcards.q: What is the magnetic field?
flashcards.a: The region around a magnet or current-carrying conductor where its magnetic effect can be felt or detected.

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: When electric current flows through a wire, a _____ is produced around the wire.
flashcards.a: Magnetic field.

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: An electromagnet becomes stronger when the number of turns in the coil is _____.
flashcards.a: Increased.

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: An electromagnet becomes stronger when the electric current flowing through it is _____.
flashcards.a: Increased.

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: A _____ wire generates more heat than a thick wire of the same material when carrying the same current.
flashcards.a: Thin.

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: Nichrome wire is used in electric heating devices because it has _____ resistance.
flashcards.a: High.

flashcards.q: Fill in the blank: A _____ cell can only be used once, while a rechargeable battery can be used many times.
flashcards.a: Dry.

flashcards.q: True or False: Electric current can produce a magnetic effect that can be detected by a compass.
flashcards.a: True.

flashcards.q: True or False: The magnetic field from a coil disappears when the electric current stops.
flashcards.a: True.

flashcards.q: True or False: A thick nichrome wire generates more heat than a thin nichrome wire when carrying the same current.
flashcards.a: False, thin wire generates more heat because of higher resistance.

flashcards.q: True or False: The poles of an electromagnet cannot be reversed.
flashcards.a: False, poles can be reversed by changing the direction of electric current.

flashcards.q: True or False: An iron core inside a coil reduces the strength of the electromagnet.
flashcards.a: False, iron core increases the strength significantly.

flashcards.q: True or False: Heat is proportional to current (H ∝ I).
flashcards.a: False, heat is proportional to the square of current (H ∝ I²).

flashcards.q: What is the right-hand rule in electromagnetism?
flashcards.a: If you curl the fingers of your right hand in the direction of current flow around a coil, your thumb points to the magnetic North pole.

flashcards.q: If the North pole of a compass is attracted to end A of a coil, what pole is end A of the electromagnet?
flashcards.a: South pole (unlike poles attract).

flashcards.q: What happens to a compass needle when a current-carrying wire is brought near it?
flashcards.a: The compass needle deflects from its original north-south direction due to the magnetic field from the wire.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Heat generated in a wire is proportional to _____, _____, and _____.
flashcards.a: Current squared (I²), resistance (R), and time (t).

flashcards.q: If you double the current through a nichrome wire, how does the heat change?
flashcards.a: Heat becomes 4 times greater (because H ∝ I², so 2² = 4).

flashcards.q: What is the formula for heat generation in a conductor?
flashcards.a: Heat = I² × R × t, where I is current, R is resistance, and t is time.

flashcards.q: True or False: A single-cell battery produces the same strength electromagnet as a 2-cell battery with the same coil.
flashcards.a: False, 2-cell battery produces a stronger electromagnet due to higher current.

flashcards.q: Can a lemon cell produce electric current?
flashcards.a: Yes, lemon juice acts as an electrolyte; a copper wire and iron nail (or zinc) serve as electrodes.

flashcards.q: How many metal pairs can be used to make a Voltaic cell?
flashcards.a: Many different pairs work, including zinc/copper, zinc/silver, aluminium/copper, iron/copper, magnesium/copper, and lead/copper.

flashcards.q: What does 'dead' mean when referring to a battery?
flashcards.a: The battery has consumed all its chemical reactants and can no longer produce electric current.

flashcards.q: Can a dead dry cell be recharged?
flashcards.a: No, dry cells cannot be recharged; once dead, they must be replaced.

flashcards.q: What is the advantage of lithium-ion batteries for modern devices?
flashcards.a: High energy density, long lifespan, fast charging, no memory effect, and lightweight design.

flashcards.q: Fill in: Earth's magnetic field is generated by the movement of _____ in the Earth's core.
flashcards.a: Liquid iron.

flashcards.q: How do migratory animals use Earth's magnetic field?
flashcards.a: They use it for navigation to find their way across continents and oceans.

flashcards.q: What are some industrial applications of the heating effect of electric current?
flashcards.a: Melting and recycling scrap steel in high-temperature furnaces.

flashcards.q: True or False: Copper wire is better than nichrome wire for heating appliances.
flashcards.a: False, nichrome wire is better because it has higher resistance and generates more heat.

flashcards.q: What is the purpose of a fuse or circuit breaker in a household circuit?
flashcards.a: To automatically disconnect the circuit if current exceeds a safe limit, preventing overheating and fires.

flashcards.q: Fill in: The connection between electricity and magnetism was discovered by _____.
flashcards.a: Hans Christian Oersted (in 1820).
